title: Entity & Fact Tracking Across Turns
concept: Extracting durable facts (name, preference, decision) from conversation and carrying them forward independent of raw history.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: in the 'Summarize-and-Forget' strategy from last lesson, what happens to older turns in the conversation?

options.text: They get compressed into a summary while recent turns stay verbatim
They are translated into a different language for storage
They are duplicated and sent twice to the model for emphasis

exercises.explain: Summarize-and-Forget periodically condenses older messages into a compact summary, while keeping the most recent turns word-for-word — giving a hybrid of long-term gist and short-term detail.

cards.title: The Case of the Forgotten Name
cards.body: You tell a chatbot your name on Monday. By Thursday, after a hundred messages, it's long gone from the recent window — but you never told it again. Should it really have forgotten? A smarter system pulls out key facts the moment they're said, so they never get lost in the shuffle.

cards.title: What Is Entity & Fact Tracking?
cards.body: It's the process of pulling out durable, reusable pieces of information — like a user's name, preferences, or decisions — from the conversation as it happens. These facts get stored separately and carried forward, so the model doesn't need the original messages to remember them.

cards.title: The Sticky Note Board
cards.body: Instead of re-reading every past conversation to remember your friend's birthday, you write it on a sticky note and stick it on a board. Next time you need it, you glance at the board — not the whole conversation history. Fact tracking is that sticky note board for your chatbot.

cards.title: Turn by Turn
cards.body: Turn 1: 'Hi, I'm Marcus.' → store name: Marcus. Turn 5: 'I hate spicy food.' → store preference: no-spicy. Turn 12: 'Let's book the 3pm slot.' → store decision: appointment=3pm. Even if turns 1-11 get deleted or summarized, those three facts stay available forever.

cards.title: Myth: 'More history = better memory'
cards.body: Keeping every raw message doesn't guarantee the model remembers what matters — a fact can get buried under thousands of irrelevant words. Explicitly extracting and storing facts is more reliable than hoping the model spots them in a giant transcript.

cards.title: Raw History vs. Fact Store
cards.body: One approach makes the model search through everything ever said; the other gives it a clean, always-current cheat sheet.

left.text: Model must scan hundreds of messages hoping to spot 'my name is...' buried somewhere in turn 47

right.text: Model checks a tiny structured record: name=Marcus, diet=vegan, appointment=3pm — instantly available, always current

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the BEST example of a 'durable fact' worth extracting and storing separately?

options.text: User's favorite color, mentioned once and unlikely to change
A filler word like 'um' the user said in turn 3
The exact timestamp the message was sent
A typo the user made and then corrected in the same message

exercises.explain: Durable facts are meaningful, reusable pieces of info — like preferences, names, or decisions — that stay relevant across many future turns. Filler words, typos, and timestamps aren't useful long-term knowledge about the user.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a summarization step (like from the previous lesson) compresses old turns away, any facts not explicitly extracted before that point could be lost forever.
exercises.explain: This is exactly why fact tracking matters alongside summarization — a summary might drop small but important details. Extracting facts as they're mentioned protects them from ever being lost, even if the raw conversation is compressed or deleted.

exercises.sentence: When a user says 'Actually, I switched jobs,' a fact-tracking system should update the stored decision instead of just appending it to the raw history.
exercises.explain: The whole point of fact tracking is that facts can change — the system should overwrite the old value with the new one, not just pile up contradictory statements in history.

exercises.prompt: Here are three fact-store entries after a long conversation. Which one looks like it was generated sloppily by an AI without proper fact management?

options.text: {name: 'Ana', diet: 'gluten-free', timezone: 'EST'} — each field holds one clean current value
{name: 'Ana', diet: 'vegetarian... wait no vegan... actually she said gluten-free too?? maybe all three', timezone: 'EST'} — the diet field is a messy pile of every version ever mentioned
{name: 'Ana', diet: 'gluten-free', timezone: 'unknown'} — a field is marked unknown because it was never mentioned

exercises.explain: The messy pile-up in option B shows a system that never resolved contradictions or updates — it just crammed every mention together instead of tracking the current, correct value. Clean fact tracking keeps one clear value per fact, updating it when new info arrives.

exercises.prompt: A user chats with a bot across many sessions: 'My name is Priya' (session 1), 'I prefer vegetarian recipes' (session 2), 'Actually, switch me to vegan' (session 3). What should the bot's fact store look like when generating a recipe in session 4?

options.text: Name: Priya; Diet preference: vegan (with vegetarian marked as outdated/overwritten)
Name: Priya; Diet preference: vegetarian AND vegan stored equally, letting the model pick randomly
No facts stored — the model should re-read all three raw sessions every time
Only the most recent message is kept: 'switch me to vegan', with the name forgotten

exercises.explain: Good fact tracking means each fact has a single current value that gets updated when the user changes their mind, while unrelated facts (like their name) stay put. The vegan preference should overwrite vegetarian, not sit alongside it — otherwise the model might contradict the user's latest wish. And you don't need the raw transcripts at all if the facts are extracted and stored properly. This wraps up Multi-Turn Memory Management: you now know how to keep chats short (summarizing), keep recent detail (hybrid windows), and keep the truth straight (fact tracking) — that's the full toolkit for engineering memory that scales.
